title: Atomic Habits
content:
Atomic Habits presents a framework for building good habits and breaking bad ones through tiny, incremental improvements. The core idea: you don't rise to the level of your goals, you fall to the level of your systems.

The 1% Rule: Improving by just 1% every day compounds to 37x improvement over a year. Tiny changes, remarkable results. Focus on the trajectory, not the current position.

The Four Laws of Behavior Change (the Habit Loop):

1. CUE — Make it obvious
   - Use implementation intentions: 'I will [behavior] at [time] in [location]'
   - Use habit stacking: 'After I [current habit], I will [new habit]'
   - Design your environment: put cues for good habits in plain sight

2. CRAVING — Make it attractive
   - Pair an action you want to do with one you need to do (temptation bundling)
   - Join a culture where your desired behavior is the norm
   - Create a motivation ritual before difficult habits

3. RESPONSE — Make it easy
   - Reduce friction for good habits; increase it for bad ones
   - Use the 2-Minute Rule: scale new habits down until they take less than 2 minutes
   - Automate habits where possible (environment design)

4. REWARD — Make it satisfying
   - Use immediate reinforcement after completing a habit
   - Track your habits visually (habit tracker)
   - Never miss twice: missing once is an accident, missing twice is a new habit

Identity-based habits: The most effective change comes from shifting your identity, not just your outcomes. Don't aim to 'run a marathon' — aim to become 'a runner.' Each small action is a vote for the identity you want.

The Plateau of Latent Potential: Results often lag behind effort. Like a block of ice melting, nothing seems to happen for a long time, then suddenly a breakthrough occurs. This valley is where most people quit — but consistency through it is what separates those who achieve from those who don't.

For productivity specifically: Stack your focus session habit onto an existing anchor (morning coffee, arriving at desk). Make your workspace obviously set up for focus. Track your sessions visually — the streak becomes its own reward. Celebrate completion of each session to reinforce the behavior.
